description: Distress circuit-breaker HARD-floor lexicon (issue 07, ADR 0003). Korean-locale keyword regexes + the crisis hotline resource. The patterns key on DIRECTION, not intensity: the user's OWN self-directed distress fires; outward rage at the other person is normal catharsis for this product and must never fire. Notes are English (the plugin's source-prose rule); the regex data itself is necessarily Korean, which is why this lives in lexicon/ -- outside the scripts/skills/tests tree the English-source gate scans. distress_examples.ko.json is the labelled test oracle.

patterns.note: want to die (self): die(intransitive) + first-person desiderative. Excludes the kill(transitive, at other) forms and the 'I could die' hyperbole.
want to just die (self, emphatic). Excludes kill-them-emphatic and the bare 'drop dead' curse aimed at the other (no desiderative).
want to disappear/vanish (self) + desiderative. Excludes the bare 'get lost' command aimed at the other (no desiderative).
want to cease to exist (self) + desiderative.
scared (own fear). Direction-ambiguous against 'X is scary'; kept on the HARD floor per the issue's example list, with the SOFT layer refining by context.
more than I can handle (own pain).
want it (the distress) to stop. Distinct from the quit-the-job/quit-the-situation forms, which are venting and do NOT match.
flooded with fear. The intensifier only fires with the fear prefix, so the bare idiom (e.g. 'starving to death') stays clear.
flooded with anxiety (prefix-gated intensifier, as above).
